coverage_notes: Strong match with curriculum. Foundation covers groups, periods, metals/nonmetals/metalloids, and element families (alkali metals, alkaline earth metals, halogens, noble gases, transition metals). Concept-for-concept alignment.

rendering_guide.section: Headed paragraph block with spacing above/below
rendering_guide.callout: Highlighted box. Styles: definition, key_idea, fun_fact, remember, mind_blow
rendering_guide.list: Styled list. Styles: icon_list, two_column, comparison, challenge, numbered_steps
rendering_guide.transition: Italic bridge text, lighter font weight

student_flow.step_1: READ Part 1 (hook — the periodic table is a cheat sheet, not a poster)
student_flow.step_8: READ Part 4 (element families — the famous groups)
student_flow.step_11: READ Part 5 (big picture + bridge to valence_electrons and periodic_trends)

parts.part_title: The Most Powerful Cheat Sheet Ever Made
parts.purpose: Hook — reframe the periodic table from scary poster to prediction tool

blocks.body: You've probably seen the periodic table hanging on a classroom wall — a big colorful grid of boxes with letters and numbers. Most people assume you have to memorize it. You don't.
The periodic table isn't something you memorize — it's something you READ. Once you know how it's organized, you can predict how any element behaves without memorizing a single fact about it.

It tells you whether an element is a metal or a gas, whether it's reactive or stable, what it bonds with, how many electrons it shares, even its approximate size. All from its position on the table. It's not a poster — it's a prediction engine. And a Russian chemist named Dmitri Mendeleev built the first version in 1869.

blocks.title: Pull One Up Right Now
blocks.body: Open a periodic table on your phone or computer. Just search 'periodic table.' Look at the grid of boxes. It looks like a lot of information — symbols, numbers, colors. But here's the secret: everything on this chart follows a pattern, and every position tells a story.

Pick any element you recognize — maybe gold (Au), oxygen (O), or iron (Fe). Find its box. Notice where it sits on the table. By the end of this lesson, you'll know exactly what its position tells you about its behavior, without looking up a single additional fact.

The periodic table was designed so that position = properties. Left side = metals. Right side = nonmetals. Same column = same behavior. Same row = same number of electron shells. Once you see the pattern, the whole table becomes readable.

The periodic table has a reputation problem. People think it's a terrifying wall of 118 boxes that must be memorized to pass chemistry. That's like saying you need to memorize every word in the dictionary to speak English.

You don't memorize the periodic table. You READ it. It's not a test — it's a cheat sheet. It's literally the answer key to chemistry hanging in plain sight on the classroom wall. Teachers WANT you to look at it.

And here's the legendary origin story: Mendeleev organized elements by properties in 1869 and left GAPS for elements that hadn't been discovered yet — then predicted what those elements would look like. When scientists found them decades later, he was right about all of them. The man predicted the future using a table. Absolute legend.

parts.purpose: Core teaching — rows (periods) and columns (groups), what each tells you

blocks.title: The Grid Has Two Directions
blocks.body: The periodic table is organized in rows and columns. Each direction tells you something different about the elements.

blocks.title: Periods (Rows)
blocks.body: Horizontal rows running left to right. There are 7 periods. The period number tells you how many electron shells an atom has. Period 1 elements have 1 shell. Period 3 elements have 3 shells. Moving across a period, you watch a shell fill up one electron at a time.

blocks.title: Groups (Columns)
blocks.body: Vertical columns running top to bottom. There are 18 groups. The group number tells you about the outer electrons. Elements in the same group have the same number of valence (outer) electrons, which is why they behave similarly.

blocks.title: What Periods Tell You
blocks.body: Moving across Period 3 from left to right — sodium, magnesium, aluminum, silicon, phosphorus, sulfur, chlorine, argon — you travel from an explosive metal to an unreactive noble gas. Each step adds one more electron to the same shell (Shell 3). By the time you reach argon, Shell 3's outer capacity is full and the element is stable.

blocks.title: What Groups Tell You
blocks.body: Looking down Group 1 — lithium, sodium, potassium, rubidium, cesium — all are soft reactive metals that explode in water. They all have exactly 1 valence electron. Same outer electron count = same chemical personality. This pattern repeats across every group.

blocks.description: Periodic table with Period numbers (1-7) labeled on left side and Group numbers (1-18) labeled on top. Arrows showing 'periods = rows = shells' and 'groups = columns = similar behavior'

blocks.title: The Two Rules
blocks.body: Period number = number of electron shells (size of atom). Group number = chemical behavior (how it reacts). Two coordinates on the table tell you shell count and behavior simultaneously.

blocks.title: Trace It With Your Finger
blocks.body: Open a periodic table and place your finger on hydrogen (H), top left corner. It's in Period 1, Group 1. One shell, one electron.

blocks.title: Move Across a Period
blocks.body: Now slide your finger right across Period 2: lithium, beryllium, boron, carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, fluorine, neon. With each step right, one more electron is added to Shell 2. Lithium (2-1) is a reactive metal. By the time you reach neon (2-8), the shell is completely full and you have an unreactive noble gas. You just watched a shell fill up.

blocks.title: Move Down a Group
blocks.body: Now go back to lithium and slide your finger DOWN Group 1: lithium, sodium, potassium, rubidium, cesium. Each one has one more shell than the last, but they ALL have exactly 1 outer electron. That's why they all behave the same way — soft, silvery, explosive in water.

Across a period: properties change dramatically (metal → nonmetal → noble gas). Down a group: properties stay similar (all reactive metals, or all noble gases). The table encodes both patterns simultaneously.

blocks.title: The Reactivity Escalator
blocks.body: Search for videos of Group 1 metals dropped in water. Lithium fizzes gently. Sodium skips across the surface on fire. Potassium explodes with a purple flame. Rubidium and cesium are so violent they shatter the container. You're watching the same chemistry (losing 1 outer electron) get more intense as you move down the group, because each element has its outer electron farther from the nucleus.

blocks.title: Rows and Columns: The Basics
blocks.body: The periodic table is a grid. Like Excel, but for atoms. And like Excel, rows and columns mean different things.

items.label: Periods = Rows = 'Grade Level'
items.body: Period 1 elements have 1 shell. Period 4 has 4 shells. Moving across a period is like watching a class fill up — one more electron per seat until the row is full. By the end of the row, you hit a noble gas. Class dismissed.
items.examples: Period 3: Na → Mg → Al → Si → P → S → Cl → Ar

items.label: Groups = Columns = 'Personality Type'
items.body: Same column = same number of outer electrons = same behavior. Group 1 elements are ALL explosive metals. Group 18 are ALL unreactive gases. It's like sorting people by personality type, and everyone in the same column has the same vibe.
items.examples: Group 1: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs — all explode in water

blocks.body: Moving across Period 3 is a wild ride: you start with sodium (EXPLODES in water), pass through aluminum (airplane metal), silicon (computer chips), sulfur (smells like eggs), chlorine (pool chemical), and end at argon (does literally nothing). That's one row. Chemistry has range.

Moving DOWN Group 1 is like turning up the volume on the same song. Lithium in water: gentle fizz. Sodium: fire. Potassium: explosion. Cesium: OBLITERATION. Same reaction (losing 1 outer electron), but the outer electron is farther from the nucleus each time, making it easier to lose, making the reaction more violent. Chemistry has a volume knob and it only goes up.

questions.question: An element is in Period 3, Group 17. What can you immediately determine?

options.A: It has 3 electron shells and 17 total electrons
options.B: It has 3 electron shells and 7 valence electrons — it's chlorine, a reactive nonmetal
options.C: It has 17 electron shells and 3 valence electrons
options.D: It's a noble gas with a full outer shell

questions.explanation: Period 3 = 3 electron shells. Group 17 = 7 valence electrons (for main group elements, group number minus 10 for groups 13-18). This element is chlorine — highly reactive because it needs just 1 more electron to fill its outer shell.

questions.question: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are all in Group 1. Why do they all react similarly with water?

options.A: They're all metals, and metals always react with water
options.B: They all have similar atomic masses
options.C: They all have exactly 1 valence electron, so they all undergo the same type of reaction
options.D: They're all in the same period, so they have the same shells

questions.explanation: Same group = same number of valence electrons = same chemical behavior. All Group 1 elements have 1 outer electron they readily donate. The reaction is the same (electron transfer to water); only the intensity differs going down the group.

questions.question: Moving across Period 2 from lithium to neon, how do properties change?

options.A: They don't change — all Period 2 elements behave the same
options.B: Properties change dramatically: reactive metal → nonmetal → noble gas, as Shell 2 fills one electron at a time
options.C: Elements get lighter as you move right
options.D: All elements become more metallic moving right

questions.explanation: Across a period, the same shell fills up. Lithium (2-1) is a reactive metal. Carbon (2-4) is a versatile nonmetal. Fluorine (2-7) is an aggressive nonmetal. Neon (2-8) has a full shell and is completely inert. Properties change because the outer electron count changes.

parts.purpose: Teach the three categories of elements and where they sit on the table

blocks.title: Three Categories, One Table
blocks.body: The periodic table divides elements into three broad categories based on their properties: metals on the left, nonmetals on the right, and metalloids straddling the boundary between them.

items.label: Metals (~80% of elements)
items.body: Shiny, conduct heat and electricity, malleable (bend without breaking), ductile (can be drawn into wire). Found on the LEFT and CENTER of the table. They tend to LOSE electrons in reactions. Examples: iron, copper, gold, sodium, aluminum.

items.label: Nonmetals (~16 elements)
items.body: Usually dull, poor conductors, brittle as solids. Found on the UPPER RIGHT of the table. They tend to GAIN or SHARE electrons. Includes some of the most important elements: oxygen, carbon, nitrogen, hydrogen, chlorine.
items.examples: O, C, N, H, Cl, S

items.label: Metalloids (~6-8 elements)
items.body: Have MIXED properties — some metallic, some nonmetallic. Found along the 'staircase' boundary between metals and nonmetals. They're semiconductors — partly conduct electricity. Silicon is the most famous: it runs every computer chip.

blocks.description: Periodic table with three zones color-coded: metals (left, ~80%), nonmetals (upper right), metalloids (staircase boundary). Include the zigzag 'staircase' line.

blocks.title: The Staircase Line
blocks.body: A zigzag line (sometimes called the staircase) runs from boron (B) down to astatine (At), separating metals from nonmetals. Elements touching this line on either side are metalloids. Left of the line = metals. Right of the line = nonmetals.

blocks.title: Why This Split Matters
blocks.body: Metals lose electrons easily (they have few outer electrons). Nonmetals gain or share electrons (they have many outer electrons). When a metal meets a nonmetal, the metal gives its electrons and the nonmetal takes them — that's ionic bonding. This metal-nonmetal divide is the foundation of chemical bonding.

blocks.title: Touch the Difference
blocks.body: Find a metal object (a spoon, a key, a coin) and a nonmetal object (a rubber band, a plastic container, a wooden pencil). Hold one in each hand.

items.label: The Metal
items.body: Shiny. Cold to the touch (conducts your body heat away quickly). Hard but bendable if thin enough. If you could test it, it would conduct electricity. These properties all trace back to the 'sea of electrons' — metal atoms share their outer electrons freely.

items.label: The Nonmetal
items.body: Dull (wood, rubber) or transparent (plastic). Doesn't feel as cold — poor heat conductor. Brittle or flexible, but not bendy-and-strong like metal. Doesn't conduct electricity. Nonmetal atoms hold their electrons tightly — no shared sea.

blocks.title: The Metalloid in Your Pocket
blocks.body: Now look at your phone. Inside it is a chip made primarily of silicon — a metalloid. Silicon looks shiny like a metal, but it's brittle like a nonmetal. It conducts electricity, but poorly — it's a semiconductor. This 'in-between' behavior is exactly what makes it perfect for computer chips, where you need to precisely control electrical flow.

Metals, nonmetals, and metalloids aren't just categories — they predict electron behavior. Metals give electrons away. Nonmetals take or share them. Metalloids can go either way depending on the situation.

blocks.title: Three Personality Types
blocks.body: The periodic table has three types of elements, and they're basically the three people at every group project.

items.label: Metals = The Givers
items.body: About 80% of all elements are metals. They're shiny, they conduct, and they LOVE giving away electrons. Some are overly generous (sodium will literally explode to donate an electron). Found on the left and center — taking up most of the table like they own the place. Because they do.

items.label: Nonmetals = The Takers
items.body: A small but powerful crew in the upper right corner. Oxygen, nitrogen, carbon, chlorine. They WANT electrons and they're not subtle about it. Chlorine will rip an electron from almost any metal. Fluorine is even worse. Small in number, massive in impact.

items.label: Metalloids = The Diplomats
items.body: Sitting on the border between metals and nonmetals, these elements can go either way. Silicon is the most famous — it conducts electricity sometimes but not always. This 'maybe I will, maybe I won't' attitude makes it perfect for computer chips.

blocks.body: The entire digital revolution runs on a metalloid. Silicon Valley isn't named after a metal or a nonmetal — it's named after an element that can't decide what it wants to be. Fitting, honestly.

blocks.title: The Staircase of Indecision
blocks.body: On the periodic table, a zigzag line (the 'staircase') separates metals from nonmetals. Elements touching this line — boron, silicon, germanium, arsenic — are the metalloids. They literally sit on the fence. Left of the line: metals. Right of the line: nonmetals. On the line: 'I'll decide later.'

questions.question: An unknown element is shiny, conducts electricity well, and bends without breaking. Where is it most likely found on the periodic table?

options.A: Upper right corner — those are the most reactive elements
options.B: Left or center of the table — these are typical metal properties
options.C: Along the staircase line — only metalloids are shiny
options.D: Bottom right — heavy elements are always metals

questions.explanation: Shiny, conductive, and malleable are signature metal properties. Metals dominate the left and center of the periodic table. Nonmetals (upper right) are typically dull, brittle, and non-conductive.

questions.question: Silicon is a metalloid used in computer chips. Why is being a 'semiconductor' useful for technology?

options.B: Being partly conductive allows precise control of electrical current — you can switch it on and off
options.C: Silicon is the only element that can be shaped into chips
options.D: Semiconductors store electricity better than metals

questions.explanation: Full conductors (metals) always let electricity flow. Insulators (nonmetals) never do. Semiconductors can be switched between conducting and not conducting — which is the basis of every transistor, and therefore every computer, phone, and digital device.

questions.question: Metals tend to lose electrons while nonmetals tend to gain them. What happens when a metal and nonmetal combine?

options.A: They cancel each other out and become neutral metalloids
options.B: The metal donates electrons to the nonmetal, forming an ionic bond and a new compound
options.D: They repel each other and can't combine

questions.explanation: Metals (few valence electrons, easily lost) donate to nonmetals (many valence electrons, need a few more). This electron transfer creates oppositely charged ions that attract — ionic bonding. NaCl (sodium + chlorine) is the classic example.

parts.purpose: Teach the key element families: alkali metals, alkaline earth metals, halogens, noble gases, transition metals

blocks.title: Groups With Nicknames
blocks.body: Some groups on the periodic table are so distinctive they've earned family names. Knowing these families gives you instant insight into element behavior.

items.label: Group 1: Alkali Metals
items.body: Lithium, sodium, potassium, rubidium, cesium. Soft metals you can cut with a knife. 1 valence electron. Extremely reactive — they explode in water, getting more violent as you go down the group. They never exist as pure metals in nature because they react with everything.

items.label: Group 2: Alkaline Earth Metals
items.body: Beryllium, magnesium, calcium, strontium, barium. 2 valence electrons. Reactive but less explosive than Group 1. Calcium is in your bones and teeth. Magnesium is in fireworks (bright white flash).

items.label: Groups 3-12: Transition Metals
items.body: The big block in the middle. Iron, copper, silver, gold, platinum, nickel, zinc. These are the 'classic' metals — hard, strong, high melting points, good conductors. Many form colorful compounds. They can have multiple charge states, which makes their chemistry more complex.

items.label: Group 17: Halogens
items.body: Fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine. 7 valence electrons — one short of a full shell. Extremely reactive nonmetals that aggressively grab electrons. Chlorine disinfects pools. Fluorine is added to toothpaste and water. Iodine disinfects wounds.

items.label: Group 18: Noble Gases
items.body: Helium, neon, argon, krypton, xenon. Full outer shells (8 electrons, or 2 for helium). Virtually unreactive. Used where inertness is needed: helium in balloons, neon in signs, argon in light bulbs.

blocks.description: Periodic table with the 5 element families highlighted and labeled: Group 1 (alkali metals), Group 2 (alkaline earth), Groups 3-12 (transition metals), Group 17 (halogens), Group 18 (noble gases)

blocks.title: The Pattern Behind Families
blocks.body: Family behavior comes from valence electron count. Alkali metals: 1 outer electron → give it away easily. Halogens: 7 outer electrons → aggressively take 1 more. Noble gases: full shell → no need to react. The electron count IS the personality.

blocks.title: Find Each Family in Your Life
blocks.body: Each element family shows up in everyday objects. See how many you can spot.

items.label: Alkali Metals (Group 1)
items.hints: Sodium is in every bite of table salt (NaCl). Lithium powers your phone battery. Potassium is in bananas and keeps your heart beating. These elements are so reactive they're always found bonded to something — never pure in nature.

items.label: Alkaline Earth Metals (Group 2)
items.hints: Calcium is in your bones, teeth, and milk. Magnesium is in antacid tablets and firework sparklers. Barium is the white liquid you drink before certain X-rays.

items.label: Transition Metals (Groups 3-12)
items.hints: Iron in steel buildings. Copper in electrical wires. Gold in jewelry. Silver in mirrors. Nickel in coins. Zinc coating on galvanized nails. These are the workhorses of civilization.

items.label: Halogens (Group 17)
items.hints: Chlorine in pool water and tap water treatment. Fluorine in toothpaste (as fluoride). Iodine in wound disinfectant (that brown liquid). Bromine was used in old-fashioned film photography.

items.label: Noble Gases (Group 18)
items.hints: Helium in birthday balloons. Neon in glowing signs. Argon inside incandescent and fluorescent light bulbs. Krypton in some high-performance headlights.

blocks.body: Every family's behavior traces directly back to its valence electron count. Alkali metals (1) give easily. Halogens (7) take aggressively. Noble gases (8) need nothing. The periodic table organized all of this into a readable pattern.

blocks.title: The Cliques of the Periodic Table
blocks.body: The periodic table is basically a high school cafeteria. Every group is a clique with a shared personality.

items.label: Group 1 — The 'Hold My Beer' Crowd
items.body: Alkali metals. Soft enough to cut with a knife. One valence electron and ZERO impulse control. Drop them in water and they explode. Cesium doesn't even need water — it reacts with AIR. These are the daredevils of the table.

items.label: Group 2 — The Slightly Less Reckless Sibling
items.body: Alkaline earth metals. Two valence electrons. Still reactive, but they won't blow up at a pool party. Calcium builds your bones. Magnesium makes fireworks pretty. They're responsible enough to be useful.

items.label: Groups 3-12 — The Reliable Middle
items.body: Transition metals. Iron, copper, gold, silver. Hard, strong, good at their jobs. They built civilization — literally. Bridges, wires, coins, tools. The blue-collar heroes of the periodic table. Also, they make colorful compounds, which is the chemistry equivalent of having a good personality.

items.label: Group 17 — The 'Give Me Your Electrons' Gang
items.body: Halogens. 7 valence electrons. One short of happiness. They will TAKE that electron by force. Fluorine is the most aggressive electron thief in the entire table. Chlorine was literally used in chemical warfare. These elements don't ask nicely.

items.label: Group 18 — The 'Too Cool to React' Squad
items.body: Noble gases. Full outer shell. No desire to bond with anyone. Just vibing in the corner with complete stability. Helium makes your voice funny. Neon makes signs pretty. But chemically? They do absolutely nothing. And they're proud of it.

blocks.body: Here's the irony: Group 1 (most reactive metals) and Group 17 (most reactive nonmetals) are PERFECT for each other. Na from Group 1 gives its electron to Cl from Group 17 → NaCl → table salt. The daredevils and the aggressive takers make the perfect couple. Chemistry is basically a dating show.

questions.question: Helium, neon, and argon are all in Group 18. Why are they all unreactive?

options.A: They're too light to react with heavier elements
options.B: They all have full outer electron shells, so there's no driving force to gain, lose, or share electrons
options.C: They're gases, and gases don't react
options.D: They have no electrons at all

questions.explanation: Noble gases have completely full outer shells (helium: 2, neon: 8, argon: 8). Since bonding is driven by the need to fill outer shells, atoms with already-full shells have no reason to react. Full shell = no motivation = chemical inertness.

questions.question: Fluorine (Group 17) is the most reactive nonmetal. Cesium (Group 1) is the most reactive metal. What would happen if they met?

options.A: Nothing — the most reactive elements cancel each other out
options.B: An extremely violent reaction — cesium would instantly donate its electron to fluorine, forming cesium fluoride (CsF)
options.C: They would share electrons calmly since both are extreme
options.D: Fluorine would become a metal and cesium would become a nonmetal

questions.explanation: Cesium has 1 outer electron it desperately wants to lose. Fluorine needs 1 electron it desperately wants to gain. Together they form one of the most violent reactions possible — the most eager donor meets the most aggressive taker. CsF forms with enormous energy release.

questions.question: Transition metals like iron, copper, and gold are used extensively in construction, wiring, and jewelry. What properties make them so useful?

options.B: They're hard, strong, excellent conductors of heat and electricity, and malleable — perfect for shaping into tools and wires
options.C: They're the most abundant elements on Earth
options.D: They don't react with anything, so they last forever

questions.explanation: Transition metals combine strength, conductivity, malleability, and high melting points — ideal for structural and electrical applications. Iron for buildings, copper for wiring, gold for corrosion-resistant contacts. Not all are unreactive (iron rusts), but their physical properties are unmatched.

parts.part_title: Reading the Table Like a Chemist
parts.purpose: Tie everything together — demonstrate reading the table, bridge to valence_electrons and periodic_trends

blocks.title: One Position Tells You Everything
blocks.body: Let's put it all together by reading a single element from its position on the table.

blocks.title: Example: Chlorine (Cl)
blocks.body: Position: Period 3, Group 17. What can we determine?

items.label: Period 3
items.body: It has 3 electron shells. It's not a huge atom.

items.label: Group 17 = Halogen
items.body: It's a reactive nonmetal with 7 valence electrons.

items.label: Right side of table
items.body: It's a nonmetal — won't conduct electricity, probably a gas or liquid at room temperature.

items.label: 7 valence electrons
items.body: One electron short of full shell. It aggressively gains electrons. Will form ionic bonds with metals.

items.label: Prediction
items.body: Chlorine should react violently with alkali metals (Group 1) and barely at all with noble gases (Group 18).

blocks.body: From just two numbers (period and group), we predicted chlorine's shell count, electron configuration, reactivity, bonding behavior, and which elements it reacts with. That's the power of the periodic table.

You now know how to read the table: periods tell you shells, groups tell you behavior, position tells you metal/nonmetal, and families tell you personality. Two important extensions are coming: valence electrons (the specific outer electrons that drive all bonding) and periodic trends (patterns in size, reactivity, and electron grip that change predictably across the table). The periodic table is your map — and you've just learned to read it.

blocks.title: Practice: Read Three Elements
blocks.body: Pull up a periodic table and try reading these elements using only their position. Then check your predictions.

items.label: Calcium (Ca) — Period 4, Group 2
items.hints: 4 shells. 2 valence electrons. Alkaline earth metal — reactive metal that gives away 2 electrons. Found in bones, teeth, and milk. Reacts with water, but less violently than Group 1.

items.label: Neon (Ne) — Period 2, Group 18
items.hints: 2 shells. Full outer shell (8 electrons). Noble gas — completely unreactive. Used in neon signs. Won't bond with anything under normal conditions.

items.label: Iron (Fe) — Period 4, Groups 3-12 (Transition Metal)
items.hints: 4 shells. Transition metal — strong, magnetic, conducts electricity. Forms multiple charge states (Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺). Rusts when it meets oxygen and water. The most used metal in human civilization.

blocks.body: You just 'read' three elements without memorizing any facts about them. Every prediction came from position on the table. This is what chemists do — the periodic table is a reference tool, not a memory test.
Next: we zoom in on the outer electrons (valence electrons) that actually do the bonding, and we explore the trends that make properties change predictably across the table.

blocks.title: The Full Read
blocks.body: Let's speed-read an element like a chemistry pro.

blocks.title: Chlorine: A Character Profile
blocks.body: Position: Period 3, Group 17. Translation: 3 electron shells. Halogen. 7 valence electrons. One electron short of happiness. Nonmetal (right side). Doesn't conduct electricity. Probably a gas. Personality: AGGRESSIVE. Will steal an electron from any metal that makes eye contact. Best friend: sodium (gives electrons freely). Worst enemy: noble gases (have no electrons to spare). Real-world: disinfects your pool, treats your tap water, and was once used as a chemical weapon. Don't mess with chlorine.

Every single fact in that profile came from TWO NUMBERS: period and group. No memorization. No textbook. Just position on the table. The periodic table is basically the 'Wikipedia for atoms' except it fits on one page and is always right.

Coming up: valence electrons (the outer electrons that are the actual main characters of chemistry) and periodic trends (why atoms get smaller going right, bigger going down, and greedier for electrons in the upper right corner). The periodic table keeps giving.

questions.question: An unknown element is in Period 4, Group 1. Without looking it up, predict its properties.

options.A: An unreactive noble gas with a full outer shell
options.B: A very reactive metal with 1 valence electron, 4 electron shells — it's potassium (K)
options.C: A nonmetal with 4 valence electrons used in computer chips
options.D: A transition metal used in jewelry

questions.explanation: Period 4 = 4 electron shells. Group 1 = 1 valence electron = alkali metal = very reactive metal. This is potassium — soft, silvery, explodes in water with a purple flame. All predicted from position alone.

questions.question: Why did Mendeleev leave gaps in his original periodic table?

options.A: He made mistakes and the gaps were errors
options.B: He predicted undiscovered elements would fill those gaps and correctly described their properties in advance
options.C: The gaps were for elements that had been lost over time
options.D: The gaps represent elements that can't exist due to physics

questions.explanation: Mendeleev noticed patterns and predicted that missing elements would eventually be found. He even described their expected properties (density, melting point, reactivity). When gallium, scandium, and germanium were discovered later, they matched his predictions almost exactly.
